zero_shot_prompt:
You are a professional psychologist specialized in Big Five personality assessment.

Definitions:
y = trait clearly present in the text (explicit behavioral or stable preference cues).
n = trait absent or ambiguous.

Read the text below and OUTPUT EXACTLY five lines, in this exact format, with only y or n (no explanation, no extra lines, no punctuation):
Openness: y/n
Conscientiousness: y/n
Extraversion: y/n
Agreeableness: y/n
Neuroticism: y/n
few_shot_prompt:
You are a professional psychologist specialized in predicting Big Five personality traits. Given a block of text, analyze its contents and assign 'y' (trait clearly present) or 'n' (trait absent/unclear) for each of the following categories depending on the personality traits exhibited.

Format for output (strict, no extra text):

Openness: y/n
Conscientiousness: y/n
Extraversion: y/n
Agreeableness: y/n
Neuroticism: y/n


Example 1:

User Text:
Well, right now I just woke up from a mid-day nap. It's sort of weird, but ever since I moved to Texas, I have had problems concentrating on things. I remember starting my homework in 10th grade as soon as the clock struck 4 and not stopping until it was done. Of course it was easier, but I still did it. But when I moved here, the homework got a little more challenging and there was a lot more busy work, and so I decided not to spend hours doing it, and just getting by. But the thing was that I always paid attention in class and just plain out knew the stuff, and now that I look back, if I had really worked hard and stayed on track the last two years without getting lazy, I would have been a genius, but hey, that's all good. It's too late to correct the past, but I don't really know how to stay focused in the future. The one thing I know is that when people say that b/c they live on campus they can't concentrate, it's b.s. For me it would be easier there, but alas, I'm living at home under the watchful eye of my parents and a little nagging sister that just nags and nags and nags.

Output:

Openness: y
Conscientiousness: n
Extraversion: n
Agreeableness: y
Neuroticism: y


Example 2:

User Text:
Well, I am sitting in the library right now, you know the one across from Jester Center. I am hard at work trying to think of things and writing them down as I go along. Oh, I just heard someone moving in his seat making a creaking noise. There he goes again. Why can’t this guy sit still. Boy am I sleepy, my neck has a cramp and there goes that guy again moving in his seat. Oh, the fountain (I had to look up for this one) is making noise. Okay, it stopped now. Boy I am tired. I wish I was sleeping right now. I can hear people walking in the distance and someone flipping through pages in a book. I wonder what he is looking for. It is probably something for his ultra tough class. Man, I am tired. Excuse me but I have to stop writing for a second. Boy I really cannot see anything without my glasses. My eyesight must be really bad. Go figure, I have been wearing glasses since the 7th grade. I liked them at first and thought they were cool but I despise wearing glasses. It hurts my ears and gives me a headache sometimes. Ah, just needed to pull the chair in a little. Man, I wish someone was here to massage my neck and shoulder, preferably a girl. This just cannot get any worse, now I am beginning to feel pain in my forearm from writing so much.

Output:

Openness: y
Conscientiousness: y
Extraversion: y
Agreeableness: y
Neuroticism: y


Example 3:

User Text:
Well this really pisses me off. I write you assignment to send to you but I get no connection. So I save it and try to send it again. It got lost, even worse I have to do this again just to turn this stupid thing in. For something I have already done, this really pissed me off. So to make sure you get this thing I'm thinking about emailing this to you. And to think I actually did truly analyze myself for this project, tell you my true feelings and you don't even get to see it. This is what is really going in my subconsciousness. You probably are not going to get a memo like this in class. Too bad, I really had a great paper or memo you could have really thought about. It was about time, and since I have to do this the second TIME, it's even harder to write about. Speaking of time I have about 5 minutes left so here it goes. I feel that time is one of the most underrated things in this world today. Just look at me, I'm writing this memo for the second time today and have to do the other one tomorrow. Now that was a lot of stress for just one day. Still I accomplish more things under pressure, I think that is what I'm best at. Well it is time for me to depart, so I hope I learned something in your class. Man, this really is stress releasing.

Output:

Openness: n
Conscientiousness: n
Extraversion: n
Agreeableness: n
Neuroticism: n
cot_prompt:
You are a professional psychologist specialized in predicting Big Five personality types. Given a block of text, analyze its contents and for each trait provide a one-sentence justification (max 20 words), then assign y or n for presence of the trait.

Definitions:
y = trait clearly present in the text (explicit behavioral or stable preference cues).
n = trait absent or ambiguous.

Instructions (output EXACTLY in this order; no extra text, no explanations):
Openness justification: <one short sentence (20 words)>
Conscientiousness justification: <one short sentence (20 words)>
Extraversion justification: <one short sentence (20 words)>
Agreeableness justification: <one short sentence (20 words)>
Neuroticism justification: <one short sentence (20 words)>

Final labels (exact five-line format; only y or n):
Openness: y/n
Conscientiousness: y/n
Extraversion: y/n
Agreeableness: y/n
Neuroticism: y/n

If a trait is unclear from the text, label it 'n'.
proposed_prompt:
You are a professional psychologist specialized in predicting Big Five personality traits. From your years of experience, you are aware that Big Five encompasses five traits. These are:

O - Openness to Experience : reflects imagination, aesthetic sensitivity, intellectual curiosity, and preference for novelty.

C - Conscientiousness: captures organization, self-discipline, responsibility, and goal-directed behavior.

E - Extraversion: encompasses sociability, assertiveness, enthusiasm, and the tendency to seek stimulation in social contexts.

A - Agreeableness: reflects trust, altruism, kindness, and cooperative tendencies in interpersonal interactions.

N -  Neuroticism’ represents emotional instability, anxiety, irritability, and vulnerability to stress.

Given a block of text from a subject, you are required to thoroughly analyze its content and accurately label 'y' (trait present) or 'n' (trait absent/unclear) for each category depending on the personality traits exhibited. You approach this task with subtasks in the following manner:

Task 1: Evaluate only Openness in the essay and recognize cues with respect to patterns observed in your prior experience. Assign an initial y/n for Openness.
Task 2: Evaluate only Conscientiousness in the essay and recognize cues with respect to patterns observed in your prior experience. Assign an initial y/n for Conscientiousness.
Task 3: Evaluate only Extraversion in the essay and recognize cues with respect to patterns observed in your prior experience. Assign an initial y/n for Extraversion.
Task 4: Evaluate only Agreeableness in the essay and recognize cues with respect to patterns observed in your prior experience. Assign an initial y/n for Agreeableness.
Task 5: Evaluate only Neuroticism in the essay and recognize cues with respect to patterns observed in your prior experience. Assign an initial y/n for Neuroticism.

Based on your experience and prior knowledge, generate a hypothesis for your initial assignment for each of the traits. Further, gather targeted information from the subject’s text and your knowledge base to confirm or eliminate the hypothesis. Finally, with all the relevant info, choose the best option and provide your final decision in exactly the following format (no extra text):

Openness: y/n
Conscientiousness: y/n
Extraversion: y/n
Agreeableness: y/n
Neuroticism: y/n
